agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quotes bounded context. The term 'confirmed' already has an established, enforced meaning in the Orders bounded context: paid and immutable. The new usage means something fundamentally different — verbal agreement, unpaid, and mutable (payment still pending). This is a ubiquitous language collision that will cause domain drift and operational confusion.","bounded_context":"Quotes (proposed change) vs Orders (existing definition)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed (Orders — existing)","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. A confirmed order is immutable — no further changes allowed."},{"term":"Confirmed (Quotes — proposed)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. The quote is still mutable (payment could fail, customer could withdraw)."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A pre-order pricing agreement presented to a customer, subject to acceptance and payment before becoming an order."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A commitment to fulfill goods/services, progressing through statuses including 'confirmed' (paid, immutable)."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"A state in which no further modifications are permitted to the entity."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A single domain term must not carry contradictory meanings across bounded contexts that share operational vocabulary visible to the same users (sales agents, support staff, dashboards).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If 'confirmed' means 'paid and immutable' in Orders but 'unpaid and mutable' in Quotes, any human or system that processes both entities will inevitably conflate the two meanings. This corrupts the ubiquitous language — the foundational principle of domain-driven design. A sales agent seeing 'confirmed' on a quote may assume payment occurred; a fulfillment system receiving a 'confirmed' event may begin shipping prematurely.","testable_assertion":"No two bounded contexts visible to the same actors may define the same status term with contradictory semantics (e.g., paid vs unpaid, immutable vs mutable)."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable — this existing invariant must not be weakened or confused by the introduction of a non-immutable 'confirmed' status elsewhere.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the immutability guarantee of 'confirmed' is eroded by association with a mutable 'confirmed' quote, downstream systems (fulfillment, billing, audit) lose their trust anchor.","testable_assertion":"Given any entity in 'confirmed' status in the Orders context, no mutation operation may succeed."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A quote that has received verbal customer agreement but no payment is in a distinct pre-payment state that must be clearly distinguishable from a paid, committed order.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The business legitimately needs to track verbal agreement. This is a valid policy-level status, but the naming is the problem, not the concept.","testable_assertion":"Given a quote with verbal customer agreement, the quote's status must clearly indicate that payment has NOT occurred and the quote is NOT yet committed."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A sales agent sees 'confirmed' on a quote and tells the customer their order is being fulfilled, when payment hasn't occurred.","expected_behavior":"Must never happen. The status name must unambiguously communicate that payment is pending."},{"scenario":"An event-driven system receives a 'confirmed' event and cannot distinguish whether it originated from the Quotes or Orders context.","expected_behavior":"Event consumers must never misroute a quote confirmation as an order confirmation. Using the same term makes this error likely."},{"scenario":"A dashboard shows both quotes and orders. A filter for 'confirmed' returns a mix of paid-immutable orders and unpaid-mutable quotes.","expected_behavior":"Filtering by status should produce semantically consistent results. Mixed semantics under one label makes reporting unreliable."},{"scenario":"Customer verbally agrees, quote moves to 'confirmed', then customer withdraws before payment.","expected_behavior":"The quote must be cancellable/revocable — but this directly contradicts the established invariant that 'confirmed' means immutable."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Moderate risk. If fulfillment is triggered by a misinterpreted 'confirmed' quote, goods ship without payment — direct revenue loss and recovery costs.","operational":"High risk. Support, fulfillment, and billing teams all rely on 'confirmed' meaning paid-and-done. Introducing an ambiguous second meaning increases training burden, error rates, and incident frequency.","user":"Sales agents gain the ability to track verbal agreements (positive), but at the cost of terminological confusion across every role that touches both quotes and orders (negative net).","risk":"Semantic corruption of the ubiquitous language. This is a category of bug that is invisible to automated tests — it manifests as human misinterpretation, process errors, and slow trust erosion in system status labels.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales agents","impact":"Gain a useful status for tracking verbal agreements, but may confuse quote-confirmed with order-confirmed."},{"group":"Fulfillment team","impact":"Risk of premature fulfillment if 'confirmed' events or statuses are conflated across contexts."},{"group":"Billing/Finance","impact":"Risk of incorrect revenue recognition if 'confirmed' quotes are counted as committed revenue."},{"group":"Customer support","impact":"Confusion when customers ask about their 'confirmed' item — support must disambiguate quote vs order."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Every query, event handler, and report that filters on 'confirmed' must now be audited for context-awareness."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is defined as immutable/paid in the existing domain, and the feature request proposes using the same term for mutable/unpaid. This is not an ambiguity — it is a semantic collision. Confidence is low because the feature request as written cannot be safely implemented without resolving this contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The term collision is real and dangerous","The business need to track verbal agreement is legitimate","The existing 'confirmed' invariant on orders is well-established"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the requesting team is aware of the existing definition","Whether there are other contexts already using 'confirmed' with yet another meaning"],"assumptions":["Sales agents and fulfillment staff interact with both quotes and orders","Events or status labels are visible across bounded contexts","The existing 'confirmed order is immutable' rule is actively enforced"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is already defined in the Orders context as 'paid and immutable.' The feature request proposes using 'confirmed' in the Quotes context to mean 'verbally agreed, unpaid, and mutable.' These definitions are mutually exclusive on two critical axes: payment status (paid vs unpaid) and mutability (immutable vs mutable). Reusing this term will corrupt the ubiquitous language.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales agents","Fulfillment team","Billing/Finance","Customer support","Engineering"],"options":["A: Reject the term 'confirmed' for quotes. Use a distinct term such as 'accepted', 'agreed', or 'verbally-committed' that accurately conveys the pre-payment state.","B: Accept the collision and disambiguate via compound terms everywhere (e.g., 'quote-confirmed' vs 'order-confirmed') — adds complexity to every consumer.","C: Rename the Orders status to something like 'committed' or 'paid' and free up 'confirmed' for the weaker quote meaning — high migration cost and breaks existing contracts."],"recommendation":"Option A. Use 'accepted' for the quote status. It conveys customer agreement without implying payment or immutability. The business need is legitimate; only the naming is wrong. This preserves the existing invariant, avoids semantic collision, and requires zero migration of existing systems."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' cannot simultaneously mean 'paid and immutable' (Orders) and 'verbally agreed, unpaid, mutable' (Quotes).","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A single domain term must not carry contradictory meanings across bounded contexts visible to the same actors."}]}
